sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ** - Board has a gray background with two white panels separated by a purple vertical bar - Left panel: dark gray box (with white interior) at lower-left, light green square with white center pixel - Between panels: light green square with black center pixel, white square - Right panel: dark gray box (with white interior) at upper area - Two green squares (same hash) appear to be agent-like objects with different inner colors (W vs B) - Goal likely involves moving green squares to interact with boxes or cross the purple barrier
- Plan: ** Probe movement to understand if green squares are controllable agents.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 2 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check the current state after the second RIGHT press. I need to understand the movement pattern better.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after 2nd RIGHT
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Board region (rows 29-33, cols 8-55):")
for r in range(29, 34):
    if r < len(ascii_lines):
        print(f"{r:3d}: {ascii_lines[r][8:56]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the pattern more clearly. After the second RIGHT:
Row 30: `gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwNNWNNNwwwppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwgg`
Row 31: `gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwNWWNBNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgg`
Row 32: `gwwcccccwwwwwwwwNNWNNNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgg`
The two green squares have moved even closer together. They seem to be merging. Let me look at the pattern:
- First green square (with white center): now at about col 24-26
- Second green square (with black center): now at about col 27-29
Wait, looking at row 31: `NWWNBN` - this looks like the two green squares have merged/overlapped. The pattern is N-W-W-N-B-N which suggests the left green square's right edge and the right green square's left edge are overlapping.
Let me think about this differently. It seems like:
1. The green squares move toward each other when RIGHT is pressed
2. They're getting closer to the purple bar
But wait - let me reconsider. Looking at the original state:
Row 30: `gwwwwwwwwwNNNwwwwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwgg`
Row 31: `gwwwwwwwwwNWNwwwwwwNBNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgg`
The left green square (N with W center) was at col ~18-20, and the right green square (N with B center) was at col ~27-29.
After 1st RIGHT:
Row 30: `gwwwwwwwwwwwwNNNwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwgg`
Row 31: `gwwwwwwwwwwwwNWNwwwNBNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgg`
Left green moved to col ~21-23, right green moved to col ~24-26. Both moved right by 3.
After 2nd RIGHT:
Row 30: `gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwNNWNNNwwwppppppwwwwwcccccwwwwwgg`
Row 31: `gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwNWWNBNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgg`
Now the left green is at col ~24-26 and right green is at col ~27-29. Both moved right by 3 again.
So pressing RIGHT moves both green squares 3 cells to the right. The dark gray box on the left also seems to have stayed in place.
The goal might be to move the green squares to specific positions or to merge them somehow. Let me try pressing RIGHT a few more times to see what happens when they reach the purple bar.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine. The green squares seem to be moving right, but the dark gray box on the left (row 32-36, col 11-15) stays in place. 
Let me try pressing LEFT to see if the squares move left.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me undo by pressing LEFT to understand movement better
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
